speaker: interviewee
text: The population divided.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to-- to first to the Gross-Rosen on the-- on the train, we were women and men together in the boxcar. But once we came to Gross-Rosen, they separated us, men separate and women separate. So that was-- we were segregated. We didn't see the women after that at all. We were just men in the barracks and everything. There was men only.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were selections made at the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: The selections? Oh, I heard-- I understand that in-- when they were liquidating the ghettos, that time they were making selections. Left goes to the camp, right goes to the-- or to execution. But then we came to-- to the concentration camp from the-- from prison. They didn't have to select us because we were-- we were dropping like flies. So whoever lived, lived. Whoever died, they just dropped. And in that concentration camp, when we came first, I didn't know what it was. There was a terrible smell. And they had the crematoriums, ovens.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what camp was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Gross-Rosen. And later on in Dora was the same thing. The smell was terrible. I couldn't-- I couldn't-- I could never get used to that. Still till today, I-- I-- I remember that-- that terrible smell, because the ovens were going day-- 24 hours a day, because they had so many people dying like flies. And they had to liquidate them. They had to-- they-- they were burning them because so many people were dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there selections ever made or were there liquidations within your barrack once you got into a barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the barracks itself was not, no. No, not in the barracks. At least I didn't go through selection. Maybe-- maybe. I-- I didn't see any selection in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody--

speaker: interviewee
text: But when every-- anybody, god forbid, got a little sick or couldn't eat, in-- in camp, they did not ask you if you don't feel good. You had to go to work. But if you didn't collect your daily ration, that time they believed you that you were sick. And most of the people didn't want to go to a sick-- or I mean to a-- a-- whatever they call, not a hospital, but-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The infirmary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Infirmary, whatever it was, because they were afraid that they would never come back. And that was exactly the truth. Once they-- they report that they are sick or they-- and went to the infirmary, they never came back. I never seen anybody came back once they said-- they-- they said that they're sick. And the same thing with-- with my father, rest in peace. It was the same thing, because he reported sick. They didn't treat it. They killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happened in Dora once you got off the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mentioned to you that they took us to the showers. They gave us-- and after the showers and they gave us new clothes, they gave us something to eat. They brought us to a mess hall, and they gave us something to eat. I couldn't eat nothing. They gave us a piece of bread, and they gave us soup. And my mouth was so dry from not eating for seven days that I-- I couldn't-- finally I had a piece of bread, and I was starving. And I couldn't eat. I just couldn't eat. But I-- I managed to-- to drink that soup a little bit. And slowly, slowly I was able to finish the soup. But I didn't have no strength, and I could not eat that piece of bread. I wrapped it, and I stuck it in my pocket. And that's where I kept the bread. And I ate it a couple days later, but I could not eat that bread. And maybe the reason because I was young at the time, and maybe I was strong enough, and I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a job in Dora?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dora, they kept us for a whole week. And just like I said, they kept us in a barrack. And we were sitting in that barrack. And the barrack was quite big. And people were dying like flies. Dead body was all over. And in the same barrack was a-- a pile of dead bodies up to the ceiling. And we were sleeping right next to it. If I think today about it, I-- I get sick. I-- but at that time, it was-- I don't know. The-- maybe the will to survive was so great that we-- we were zombies. We didn't know what's going on. And we looked at dead bodies, and it didn't-- didn't phase us. It didn't. It just-- I can't-- I can't-- it's hard to explain what-- what people go through. And it's hard to-- to-- to visualize how you can sit in the same room with-- with hundreds of bodies right-- right next to you. But about four-- about a week later, they took us out from the barrack, whoever was able to walk, and they picked us out. And they picked me, maybe the reason because I was still walking. And they took us to a little-- on the truck-- to a little labor camp. And the labor-- labor camp was Osterode. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First, yeah, on the truck, they took us to the labor camp in Osterode. The condition was much better in that place because we had separate barracks, and they were fortunate to get a bunk. And we were about 40 people in a barrack. And everybody had a bunk to sleep on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your own bunk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Your own bunk. That was the first time that I came to a camp where they gave me my own bunk to sleep on. And we were over there. So they took us every morning, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] walking maybe three, four miles to woods. It was cold and snowy. And we had wooden shoes on. Wooden shoes-- the shoes were with a wooden sole-- sole-- I mean, a leather top, but a wooden sole. And when we were walking through the snow, the snow used to stick-- get stuck to the wooden sole. And we were walking like on stilts because the snow was-- so we had to stop every little while and knock off the snow from the shoes. And the walking wasn't as bad as knocking off each time there was heavy, because the snow, the shoes were heavy. And that was like a torture almost. And we were walking for-- for over two weeks, I think. We had to go every morning to the woods. And everything was frozen and cold. And they gave us a-- a-- a pick and shovel. And we had to dig out old tree stumps. And everything was frozen. So actually didn't have no-- the reason-- there was no reason for digging up those stumps because those were frozen. But it seems like they-- they just wanted to punish us or something. After two weeks in that labor thing I was going through, they picked me to a factory, which were making parts-- airplane parts. And it was a great thing for me because I didn't have to be on the outsides in the woods. And I was making airplane parts. There was an assembly line. And in that assembly line, we were making airplane parts. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Condition were not great. But it was much better. We had guards over us and yelling at us and watching us over. And-- but there was still much better conditions than going to-- every morning to the woods and digging the stumps out of the frozen ground, at that-- at that-- that cold weather too. So that's what I was at the-- at that-- at that factory, until-- until April. And gets to spring was a little better. And in spring-- I mean in April [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 8-- no, April 10-- April 10-- there was a few days before my birthday. They loaded us on the trucks. And they never tell us where we're going. And we didn't know where we're going. And each time they just tell us that we, just like cattle, listen to what they-- what they-- what we have to do. And we followed them because all that was us-- guards with guns and things. So we listened. They loaded us on the trucks, open trucks. And we were on the road for maybe three, four hours. And we didn't know where we're going. And all of sudden the trucks stopped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the guards disappeared. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we didn't know what happened. And before we know, there was American tanks came in. And naturally, the first reaction was, we jumped off the trucks, and we felt that we are free. The American troops are here.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people do you think there were in those trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In those trucks--

speaker: interviewer
text: The prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: --might have been maybe around 200. It was like three, four trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me actually how they celebrated? How did these people celebrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we-- we were beside ourselves, you know, that all of the sudden, the guards disappeared. And we feel that we are free. And the first thing, we were starving. So the first thing what we did, we went-- because there was a village, and we went to some German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] farmers. And we asked for some food. And they didn't know who we were, but they gave us some food. Because they knew that the Americans were coming. And because I spoke German, so I could talk to them. And I-- me and a couple of my buddies got into a German house-- farmer's house, and we asked for some food. And not that they wanted to give us, but we requested it. [LAUGHS] And they were afraid that they might-- that we might do something. I-- I was no condition to do anything to anybody. I-- I was so weak. And we were always hungry. So all those times we always were hungry, because we didn't get much-- much food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed with the farmer for two days in that house. And I'm going out on the third day. I'm going out for fresh air out of the farm house to the street. And all of the sudden, I see the German tanks are coming back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on-- on one of the German tanks was an American soldier on the front, was wounded. And they put him right on the-- on the tank in order to prevent, maybe, from the Americans tank that they should shoot-- shoot them. So they kept an American soldier wounded, tied down, as a shield on their tank.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when-- my-- I-- my feet was, from that wooden shoes and walking through the woods there, my feet was full of sores. And I hardly could walk because they-- we didn't have no socks, just bare-- bare shoes. And when I saw that the German tanks are coming, and there was like maybe 50 feet, a hundred feet away, I start running. And I saw the American trucks running-- I mean, loading few people and driving off. And one of my buddy, who walked out maybe 10 minutes earlier to the street, he was running after that truck, after the American soldiers' truck. And they picked him up. They took him, on the truck. And I was running after the truck, and they just drove away. And I said no, I'm not gonna go back, regardless of what's going to happen. If they have to shoot me, I'm not going back to the camp. And I kept running in the same direction what the trucks went. Now the reason why what happened, what-- I believed that we were liberated by Patton troops. Patton had a-- his way of-- was that he was always advancing, always to the front advancing. Keep on going. And he did not clean out the pockets of German resistance. And a few-- the three tanks hid-- hid in the woods. So the main American force kept on going, but they did not clean out the pockets of the resistance. So that was the reason why the three tanks came out. And I kept running in the direction where the American trucks went. And maybe a couple of miles later, I see more American troops. And they asked me if I seen German. And so I couldn't speak English, but whatever I told them, I told them. I said the three tanks, German tanks were there. So I seen that they were aware of it, and I knew that I was safe. I was behind the American lines. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's how I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever remember when you were running how your feet felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know you said something before I wanted to go back. You said that they treated you like Jews. Tell me what you meant by that.

speaker: interviewee
text: To treat us as Jews, [DRINKS] we were not people. We were useful, but we were not people. If a German-- any German-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] decides to shoot a Jew just for the sake of it, there was-- there was very common, regardless of where or what situation or where it was. In fact, during my being in the ghetto, occasionally were-- were some Gestapo people which I knew very well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was-- very-- that just reminded me of something, what I tell you happened after the war. And I lead to it. That-- there was, on the Gestapo headquarters, was one guy who was very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a short fellow. And he was limping. He had one foot short. And he was in-- in charge of-- of catching resistance, because there was Polish resistance, Jewish resistance. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was one of the meanest Gestapo guy I-- we knew. He was very mean. He used to shoot people. He used to beat up people. And he was very mean. And many times, we heard that he used to go into the-- into the ghetto, and when he saw a Jewish man who was built nice or good looking or strong looking, he'd shoot them on the spot. And he-- he used to say, at least now I'm shooting you because someday you might be a-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a person who-- who-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who might be my enemy. So he-- he used to-- he used to shoot people right on the street. And there was-- there was just like-- going to-- to have a pleasure or something. And he used to go to ghetto and shoot a few-- a few-- a few Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Von Malutschky. Very [INAUDIBLE]. I tell you what happened. It happened that after the war, I used to live in Celle, not far from Hamburg-- Hamburg, the-- the city-- port of Hamburg. And we went there. In fact, I went there to sight seeing. And we were-- there was in 1945, in September, October, I was there on the train station. And I came across of him. I recognized him. I recognized him. I summoned the British because there was the British MP that time. And I told them that he's an ex-Nazi, and he was ex-- ex-Gestapo man. And they-- they arrested him. And he was, later on I found out, he was deported. I mean, not deported, it was-- he was-- they gave him out to Poland. And he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Expedited.

speaker: interviewee
text: --expedited. He was expedited to Poland as a war-- war criminal. And he was executed in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you put the heat on? It's cold here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't want to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you don't want the heat on?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's OK. Tell me about when you got on the truck, after you got on the truck with the Americans. You were running towards the Americans, and you got on the truck. Then what happened? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- I ran. I didn't know where I was running. I was running away from-- from the place [LAUGHS] where the German tanks were. And I wound up in the small little town-- little town-- of Gifhorn-- Gifhorn. That was not far from Braunschweig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gifhorn, that's G-I-F-H-O-R-N, Gifhorn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there when I came in, first thing we were looking for some shelter. And the Germans were very much aware of what's going on. And when they saw somebody, especially I had the-- my concentration uniform on. They took-- there was a little rooming house. And I stopped at that rooming house. And he gave-- says come in, come in. Because he wanted to show maybe later on to the-- to the-- that he was helping somebody. And he took me into the-- and he gave me a room. I was with my-- I met a buddy of mine, which we were in the same camp. And he took us to the-- and he gave us a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your friend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul Winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Paul Winter. Yeah. Paul Winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in the rooming house?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were-- we were in the rooming house. And we started-- so we had a shelter. We had a room to stay-- stay there. And once we were there, we tried to get some-- we went to authority. The American-- that was English-- English authority, because they were in charge over there. And we went to-- to Rathaus. That was the-- the office-- office of the mayor. And-- and we got our ration cards. And we got food. We got-- we become people.
